narrative:
Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 48% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, your LSAT score of 31, your Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings of 1st and 2nd respectively, and details like your upper-middle family income and full-time study plans. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is much higher at 37.27, and typical decile rankings sit around 6th for both early and later years, highlighting how your profile stacks up against those who succeeded.

The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your undergraduate GPA, your Year 1 decile, and your Year 3 decile, in that order of influence. Your solid undergraduate GPA stood out positively, as it's above the 3.24 average for bar passers, and it nudged the model's assessment toward expecting you to pass rather than fail. In contrast, your low 1st-place decile in Year 1—far below the 6th-place average for successful students—strongly pushed the prediction toward failure, signaling early academic challenges in a competitive law environment.

Similarly, your 2nd-place decile in Year 3, again well below the typical 6th-place mark for those who passed, reinforced that concern and contributed toward the failure prediction. While your upper-middle family income aligns with about 45% of bar passers and your full-time status matches over 93% of them, these didn't carry enough weight to offset the early performance signals. To move forward, focus on boosting your LSAT through targeted prep—many students improve significantly with practice—and consider programs or experiences that demonstrate upward academic trends, then reapply with a stronger profile to shift future predictions in your favor.
